message:
fix(security,reports): harden integrity hashing, delimiter safety, and content hash coverage

Incident bundle retention (incident_bundle_retention.rs):
- Expand compute_integrity_hash() to cover size_bytes, created_at_epoch,
  and last_tier_change_epoch, preventing hash bypass when retention-
  affecting fields are tampered with after initial bundle creation
- Recompute integrity_hash after tier rotation so exported bundles always
  carry a hash that matches their current state
- Add 3 tests verifying each new field is covered by the hash
- Add test verifying export succeeds after tier rotation with correct
  integrity hash
- Add integrity hash consistency assertions to existing tier transition tests

Remote capability tokens (security/remote_cap.rs):
- Replace comma-delimited join in canonical_payload() and
  scope_fingerprint() with length-prefixed encoding via
  encode_scope_entries(), preventing operations or endpoint prefixes
  containing commas from producing ambiguous token signatures
- Add tests: issued_caps_preserve_endpoint_prefix_boundaries verifies
  distinct tokens for ["alpha,beta","gamma"] vs ["alpha","beta,gamma"];
  issuing_identical_endpoint_prefix_scopes_is_deterministic verifies
  same input produces same token

Transparent reports (tools/transparent_reports.rs):
- Extract compute_report_content_hash() using structured serde
  serialization of all mutable report fields (report_id, title,
  category, severity, sections, timeline, root_causes,
  corrective_actions, lessons_learned, report_version, created_at)
  instead of ad-hoc JSON object with only title/category/sections
- Extract compute_catalog_content_hash() covering by_severity and
  open_actions in addition to existing by_category and report_version
- Recompute content_hash after add_corrective_action() and
  update_action_status() mutations, emitting TR_INTEGRITY_VERIFIED
  audit events
- Add 5 tests: severity changes hash, timeline changes hash, add
  action refreshes hash, update status refreshes hash, catalog hash
  changes with open actions

Verification scripts:
- check_incident_bundles.py: add C35 check verifying integrity hash
  covers retention fields
- check_transparent_reports.py: strengthen content_hashing check to
  verify all report fields; add catalog_hashing check for by_severity
  and open_actions coverage
- tests: update expected check counts and add assertions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"bd-1a5bl","title":"Seal incident-bundle retention integrity over all stored fields","description":"Why this exists: docs/policy/incident_bundle_retention.md says integrity_hash is computed over the canonical JSON representation of all bundle fields except integrity_hash itself, but crates/franken-node/src/connector/incident_bundle_retention.rs compute_integrity_hash currently omits stored fields such as created_at_epoch, last_tier_change_epoch, and size_bytes. That means retention-driving or storage-accounting state can be mutated without invalidating the integrity check, violating INV-IBR-INTEGRITY and the policy contract. What this task must do: make the retention integrity hash and validation path cover every behaviorally significant stored field, or explicitly restructure the stored/exported split so the invariant is honest and enforceable. Add focused regressions that prove tampering with previously omitted fields is detected. Acceptance criteria: 1. The integrity implementation and docs agree on which fields are hash-covered. 2. Mutating created_at_epoch, last_tier_change_epoch, and size_bytes after hashing causes integrity validation or store rejection, or an equivalent fail-closed signal. 3. Regression tests lock the behavior down. 4. Cargo-heavy validation is run through rch and summarized in Agent Mail.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonBasin","created_at":"2026-03-15T15:34:34.966933555Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-15T16:14:42.719140422Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T16:14:42.719107351Z","close_reason":"Implemented; targeted rch validation passed, workspace-wide rch checks hit infra limits","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["incident-bundle","integrity","policy","retention"]}

 {"id":"bd-1ah","title":"[10.4] Define provenance attestation requirements and verification chain.","description":"## Why This Exists

The extension ecosystem (10.4) requires cryptographic provenance as its trust anchor. Without verifiable attestation of who built an extension, from what source, using which toolchain, and under what conditions, the entire trust-card, reputation, and quarantine system has no ground truth. Provenance attestation is the second foundational layer after the manifest schema (bd-1gx): the manifest declares *what* an extension is; provenance proves *where it came from and how it was built*.

This bead defines the requirements and verification chain that every extension artifact must satisfy before admission into the registry or execution by the runtime. It connects directly to the three-kernel trust model where `franken_node` must independently verify extension provenance claims rather than trusting upstream assertions.

## What This Must Do

1. Define the provenance attestation schema including: source repository URL, commit hash, build system identifier, builder identity, reproducibility markers (build environment hash, input hash, output hash), timestamp, and attestation signature.
2. Define the verification chain model: who signs what, in what order, what revocation semantics apply to each link, and how partial chain breaks are handled.
3. Specify the attestation format compatible with in-toto / SLSA provenance standards adapted for the franken_node extension ecosystem.
4. Define minimum provenance levels (e.g., Level 0: unsigned, Level 1: signed by publisher, Level 2: signed + reproducible build claim, Level 3: signed + independently reproduced) and map them to policy admission gates.
5. Define the provenance verification algorithm: input = manifest + attestation bundle, output = provenance level + chain validity report.
6. Specify integration points with the manifest schema (bd-1gx) for embedding provenance references and with the revocation system (bd-12q) for revocation of compromised attestation keys.
7. Define degraded-mode behavior when provenance verification infrastructure is unavailable (fail-closed vs. cached-trust windows).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Provenance schema is published as both human-readable spec and machine-readable JSON Schema.
- Verification chain algorithm is specified with deterministic outputs for all input classes (valid, expired, revoked, partial, missing).
- At least 4 provenance levels are defined with clear mapping to policy gates.
- Attestation format is compatible with SLSA v1.0+ provenance predicates.
- Schema includes extension points for future attestation types without breaking existing verifiers.
- Integration contract with bd-1gx manifest schema is explicit (field references, embedding rules).
- Degraded-mode behavior is specified with explicit fail-closed defaults for high-risk operations.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: provenance schema validation (valid attestations, malformed attestations, missing fields, expired signatures, revoked keys, chain gap scenarios).
- Integration tests: manifest + attestation bundle creation -> verification -> level assignment pipeline.
- Adversarial tests: forged attestations, key substitution attacks, replay of old attestations against new artifacts.
- Structured logs: PROVENANCE_VERIFIED, PROVENANCE_REJECTED (with reason code), PROVENANCE_LEVEL_ASSIGNED, PROVENANCE_CHAIN_BROKEN, PROVENANCE_DEGRADED_MODE_ENTERED. All logs include trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixtures for each provenance level and failure mode.

## Expected Artifacts

- `docs/specs/section_10_4/bd-1ah_contract.md` — provenance attestation requirements spec
- `schemas/provenance_attestation.schema.json` — machine-readable attestation schema
- `src/supply_chain/provenance.rs` — Rust types implementing provenance verification
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-1ah/verification_evidence.json` — CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-1ah/verification_summary.md` — human-readable outcome summary

## Dependencies

- **bd-1gx** (blocks this) — manifest schema defines the structure provenance attests to
- Blocked by: bd-1gx
- Blocks: bd-261k (section gate), bd-2ac (secure extension distribution), bd-1xg (plan tracker), bd-2yh (trust cards need provenance data)","acceptance_criteria":"1. Provenance attestation schema defines required fields: source repository URL, build system identifier, builder version, reproducibility hash, VCS commit SHA, build timestamp, and SLSA provenance level claim.
2. Verification chain model supports transitive attestation: publisher attests build, build system attests source, source VCS attests commit integrity. Each link carries a cryptographic signature.
3. Attestation verification is fail-closed: missing or invalid attestation at any chain link rejects the extension with a structured error identifying the broken link and required remediation.
4. Attestation format supports both in-toto and custom franken_node envelope formats, with a canonical serialization for deterministic signature verification.
5. Verification chain depth is configurable per policy profile (e.g., dev profile may accept self-signed, prod requires full chain).
6. Attestation freshness is enforced: stale attestations (older than configurable threshold) trigger re-verification or rejection.
7. Integration point with 10.13 FCP supply chain gates: attestations are consumed by threshold-signature and transparency-log verification modules.
8. All verification decisions emit structured log events: ATTESTATION_VERIFIED, ATTESTATION_REJECTED, CHAIN_INCOMPLETE, CHAIN_STALE with provenance metadata and trace IDs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SunnyPond","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:45.507060902Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T17:46:06.446787554Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T17:46:06.446754372Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-4","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ah","depends_on_id":"bd-1gx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:13:28.637233667Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ayu","title":"[10.14] Implement overhead/rate clamp policy for hardening escalations with configured ceilings.","description":"## Why This Exists
While automatic hardening triggers (bd-1zym) ensure the system responds to threats, uncontrolled escalation can itself become a problem — rapid escalation consumes resources, disrupts normal operations, and can cause cascading performance degradation. The overhead/rate clamp policy sets deterministic ceilings on how fast and how much the system can harden within a given time window and resource budget. This prevents "escalation storms" where a burst of guardrail violations causes the system to slam to maximum hardening faster than operators can react. Inspired by FrankenSQLite's I/O rate limiter for checkpoint operations (9J enhancement map), this supports Section 8.5 Invariant #9 (bounded resource consumption) by ensuring that even protective mechanisms operate within predictable resource bounds.

## What This Must Do
1. Implement `HardeningClampPolicy` in `crates/franken-node/src/policy/hardening_clamps.rs` with:
   - `fn check_escalation(proposed: HardeningLevel, current: HardeningLevel, budget: &EscalationBudget) -> ClampResult` — evaluates whether the proposed escalation is within rate and overhead bounds.
   - `EscalationBudget` struct: `max_escalations_per_window: u32`, `window_duration: Duration`, `max_overhead_pct: f64` (maximum additional CPU/memory overhead from hardening), `min_level: HardeningLevel`, `max_level: HardeningLevel`.
   - `ClampResult` enum: `Allowed`, `Clamped { effective_level: HardeningLevel, reason: String }`, `Denied { reason: String }`.
2. Make clamp calculations deterministic:
   - Given identical input state (current level, budget, escalation history), the clamp produces identical output.
   - No randomness, no system-clock jitter dependency (use monotonic counters).
3. Enforce min/max bounds:
   - Escalation cannot go below `min_level` (policy floor) or above `max_level` (policy ceiling).
   - Rate limit: no more than `max_escalations_per_window` escalations within `window_duration`.
   - Overhead limit: estimated overhead of target level cannot exceed `max_overhead_pct`.
4. Make clamp hits visible in telemetry:
   - `ClampEvent` struct: `timestamp`, `proposed_level`, `effective_level`, `reason`, `budget_utilization_pct`.
   - Clamp events are emitted as structured telemetry and recorded in the evidence ledger.
5. Write conformance tests at `tests/conformance/hardening_clamp_bounds.rs` covering:
   - Rate limit enforcement (exceed max escalations within window).
   - Overhead limit enforcement (target level overhead exceeds budget).
   - Min/max bound enforcement.
   - Deterministic calculation verification.
6. Produce metrics artifact at `artifacts/10.14/hardening_clamp_metrics.csv` with columns: `timestamp`, `proposed_level`, `effective_level`, `clamp_reason`, `budget_utilization_pct`, `rate_count`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Escalation clamps respect min/max bounds and policy budget; clamp calculations are deterministic; clamp hits are visible in telemetry.
- Rate limit correctly blocks escalations exceeding `max_escalations_per_window`.
- Overhead limit correctly caps escalation when estimated overhead exceeds `max_overhead_pct`.
- Min/max bounds are always enforced regardless of trigger urgency.
- Same inputs produce same `ClampResult` across 1000 runs.
- Clamp events appear in telemetry CSV with all required fields.
- `Clamped` result provides the actual effective level (possibly lower than proposed but higher than current).

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: Each `ClampResult` variant with boundary inputs; rate limit at exactly N, N+1 escalations; overhead calculation at limit; deterministic calculation with fixed inputs; budget with `max_level < proposed` triggers clamping; budget with `min_level > current` handles gracefully.
- Integration tests: Full trigger -> clamp -> effective escalation flow; sustained escalation attempts to verify window-based rate limiting; overhead measurement with known hardening level costs.
- Conformance tests: Determinism across 1000 identical inputs; rate window rollover (escalations in previous window do not affect current window); clamp metrics CSV is valid and parseable.
- Adversarial tests: Zero-duration window (divide-by-zero protection); `max_overhead_pct = 0.0` (blocks all escalation); `max_escalations_per_window = 0` (blocks all escalation); rapid alternation of escalation/clamp cycle.
- Structured logs: `EVD-CLAMP-001` on escalation allowed; `EVD-CLAMP-002` on escalation clamped (includes effective level, reason); `EVD-CLAMP-003` on escalation denied; `EVD-CLAMP-004` on budget recalculated. All logs include `epoch_id`, `budget_utilization_pct`.

## Expected Artifacts
- `crates/franken-node/src/policy/hardening_clamps.rs` — implementation
- `tests/conformance/hardening_clamp_bounds.rs` — conformance tests
- `artifacts/10.14/hardening_clamp_metrics.csv` — clamp metrics
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1ayu/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable pass/fail evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1ayu/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary

## Dependencies
- Upstream: bd-3rya (hardening state machine — provides the current level and escalation API)
- Downstream: bd-1fp4 (integrity sweep scheduler uses clamp-aware escalation), bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (10.14 plan gate)","acceptance_criteria":"Escalation clamps respect min/max bounds and policy budget; clamp calculations are deterministic; clamp hits are visible in telemetry.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:56.219100534Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T18:34:33.851069590Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T18:34:33.851039925Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ayu","depends_on_id":"bd-3rya","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:23:52.975925001Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1fi2","title":"[10.8] Section-wide verification gate: comprehensive unit+e2e+logging","description":"## Why This Exists

This is the section-wide verification gate for Section 10.8 (Operational Readiness). Section 10.8 ensures franken_node is ready for production deployment: fleet control APIs, structured observability, safe-mode operation, incident bundle retention, operator runbooks, and disaster-recovery drills. This gate validates that all operational readiness capabilities meet production standards.

Section 10.8 is the difference between "works in the lab" and "works in production." Fleet control, incident response, and disaster recovery are what operators need when things go wrong at scale. This gate ensures those capabilities are tested under realistic failure scenarios, not just happy-path conditions.

## What This Must Do

1. Aggregate verification evidence from all 6 Section 10.8 beads:
   - bd-tg2: Implement fleet control API for quarantine/revocation operations
   - bd-3o6: Adopt canonical structured observability + stable error taxonomy contracts
   - bd-k6o: Implement deterministic safe-mode startup and operation flags
   - bd-f2y: Implement incident bundle retention and export policy
   - bd-nr4: Implement operator runbooks for high-severity trust incidents
   - bd-3m6: Implement disaster-recovery drills for control-plane failures
2. Verify fleet control coverage: quarantine and revocation operations work across multi-node deployments.
3. Verify observability completeness: all control-plane events emit structured logs with stable error codes and trace correlation.
4. Verify safe-mode operation: deterministic startup in safe-mode with documented capability restrictions.
5. Verify DR drill execution: at least one disaster-recovery drill has been executed with documented results.
6. Produce deterministic gate verdict.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All 6 section beads must have PASS verdicts.
- Fleet control API tested under simulated multi-node scenarios.
- Observability audit confirms 100% event coverage with stable codes.
- Safe-mode startup tested and documented.
- At least 1 DR drill executed with pass/fail results documented.
- Gate verdict is deterministic and machine-readable.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Gate self-test with mock evidence.
- Structured logs: GATE_10_8_EVALUATION_STARTED, GATE_10_8_BEAD_CHECKED, GATE_10_8_OPS_READINESS_AUDITED, GATE_10_8_VERDICT_EMITTED.

## Expected Artifacts

- `scripts/check_section_10_8_gate.py` — gate script with `--json` and `self_test()`
- `tests/test_check_section_10_8_gate.py` — unit tests
- `artifacts/section_10_8/bd-1fi2/verification_evidence.json`
- `artifacts/section_10_8/bd-1fi2/verification_summary.md`

## Dependencies

- Blocked by: bd-tg2, bd-3o6, bd-k6o, bd-f2y, bd-nr4, bd-3m6, bd-1dpd, bd-2twu
- Blocks: bd-2j9w (program-wide gate), bd-c4f (plan tracker)","acceptance_criteria":"1. Section 10.8 gate aggregates pass/fail status from all sibling beads (bd-tg2, bd-3o6, bd-k6o, bd-f2y, bd-nr4, bd-3m6).
2. Gate script (scripts/check_section_10_8_gate.py) runs all section verification scripts and produces a unified JSON report.
3. Gate fails if any sibling bead's verification evidence is missing or shows a failure.
4. Unit tests cover gate logic: all-pass, single-fail, and missing-evidence scenarios.
5. Gate produces a section-level summary under artifacts/ with per-bead status, timestamps, and links to individual evidence files.
6. E2E logging: gate execution emits structured log lines for each sub-check with bead ID, result, and duration.
7. Gate is idempotent: running it twice in succession with no changes produces identical output.","notes":"Root-cause addendum: this gate must include corrupted-checkpoint, stale-frontier, crash-loop, and epoch-mismatch safe-mode drills; verify operator-facing safe-mode receipts, blocked-capability reporting, and clearance receipts; and validate stable SAFE_MODE_* logs plus at least one deterministic tests/e2e shell drill with captured rch verification IDs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","owner":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.005618085Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:17:04.561431342Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T07:17:04.561324553Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-8","test-obligations","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-1dpd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T08:24:24.596033499Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-2twu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T08:20:50.133799070Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-3ly7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T23:14:44.173326817Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-3m6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.101845374Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-3o6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.310271429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-f2y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.195563990Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-k6o","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.255849585Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-nr4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.148262809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","depends_on_id":"bd-tg2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:26.360765476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":145,"issue_id":"bd-1fi2","author":"RoseMountain","text":"Reopened after code-level archaeology found placeholder safe-mode trust verification and fake duration arithmetic in crates/franken-node/src/runtime/safe_mode.rs. New gap-closure bug bd-3ly7 now blocks truthful section closure.","created_at":"2026-03-08T23:14:46Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1fp4","title":"[10.14] Implement integrity sweep escalation/de-escalation policy driven by evidence trajectories.","description":"## Why This Exists
franken_node periodically performs integrity sweeps — full scans that verify object integrity, check protection artifact completeness, and detect silent corruption. But running sweeps at a fixed cadence is wasteful: during calm periods sweeps run too often, and during active threat periods they run too infrequently. The integrity sweep scheduler dynamically adjusts sweep cadence based on evidence trajectories — patterns in recent guardrail rejections, hardening escalations, and repairability scores. When evidence suggests increasing risk, sweeps escalate (more frequent, more thorough). When evidence shows sustained stability, sweeps de-escalate (less frequent, lower overhead). Hysteresis prevents oscillation between escalated and de-escalated states. Inspired by FrankenSQLite's adaptive checkpoint scheduler (9J enhancement map), this supports Section 8.5 Invariant #9 (bounded resource consumption) by ensuring sweep overhead scales with actual risk rather than running at maximum all the time.

## What This Must Do
1. Implement `IntegritySweepScheduler` in `crates/franken-node/src/policy/integrity_sweep_scheduler.rs` with:
   - `fn update_trajectory(evidence: &EvidenceTrajectory) -> &mut Self` — incorporates new evidence into the trajectory model.
   - `fn next_sweep_interval(&self) -> Duration` — returns the recommended interval until the next sweep.
   - `fn current_sweep_depth(&self) -> SweepDepth` — returns the current sweep thoroughness (Quick, Standard, Deep, Full).
   - `EvidenceTrajectory` struct: `recent_rejections: u32`, `recent_escalations: u32`, `avg_repairability: f64`, `trend: Trend` (Improving, Stable, Degrading).
2. Implement evidence band thresholds:
   - Define `PolicyBand` thresholds for escalation/de-escalation: `green` (stable), `yellow` (concern), `red` (active threat).
   - Band transitions drive cadence changes: green = long intervals/quick sweeps, yellow = medium/standard, red = short/deep.
3. Implement escalation/de-escalation hysteresis:
   - De-escalation requires N consecutive evidence updates in a lower band before cadence decreases (prevents oscillation).
   - Escalation is immediate (one evidence update in a higher band triggers cadence increase).
   - Hysteresis threshold N is policy-configurable.
4. Record all scheduling decisions in the evidence ledger:
   - `SweepScheduleDecision` struct: `timestamp`, `band`, `interval`, `depth`, `trajectory_summary`, `hysteresis_count`.
5. Write performance tests at `tests/perf/integrity_sweep_adaptation.rs` covering:
   - Cadence increases when evidence degrades.
   - Cadence decreases (with hysteresis) when evidence improves.
   - Oscillation prevention — rapid band changes do not cause cadence flickering.
   - Sweep overhead stays within configured resource budget.
6. Produce trajectory artifact at `artifacts/10.14/sweep_policy_trajectory.csv` with columns: `timestamp`, `band`, `interval_ms`, `depth`, `rejection_count`, `escalation_count`, `repairability_avg`, `hysteresis_count`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Sweep cadence adjusts according to policy evidence bands; escalation/de-escalation hysteresis prevents oscillation; decisions are ledgered.
- Cadence increases immediately on evidence degradation (yellow->red triggers within one update).
- Cadence decreases only after N consecutive stable updates (hysteresis).
- Sweep depth increases with cadence (short intervals = deeper sweeps).
- Oscillation test: alternating good/bad evidence does not cause cadence to oscillate faster than hysteresis allows.
- All scheduling decisions appear in evidence ledger with trajectory context.
- Trajectory CSV artifact is valid, parseable, and shows band transitions.

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: Band classification for known trajectories; interval calculation at each band; hysteresis counter increment/reset; sweep depth selection for each band; edge case — first update (no history); trajectory with all zeroes.
- Integration tests: Full evidence stream -> scheduler adaptation -> verify cadence changes; sustained stable evidence -> de-escalation after N updates; burst of rejections -> immediate escalation; verify evidence ledger contains scheduling decisions.
- Conformance tests: Hysteresis prevents oscillation over 1000 alternating updates; cadence monotonically decreases during sustained improvement; cadence monotonically increases during sustained degradation; scheduling decisions are deterministic given identical trajectory.
- Adversarial tests: Trajectory with NaN/Inf values; extremely high rejection count (overflow protection); hysteresis threshold of 0 (should degrade to no hysteresis); concurrent trajectory updates from multiple sources.
- Structured logs: `EVD-SWEEP-001` on sweep scheduled (includes interval, depth, band); `EVD-SWEEP-002` on band transition (includes from/to band); `EVD-SWEEP-003` on hysteresis preventing de-escalation; `EVD-SWEEP-004` on trajectory updated. All logs include `epoch_id`, `hysteresis_count`.

## Expected Artifacts
- `crates/franken-node/src/policy/integrity_sweep_scheduler.rs` — implementation
- `tests/perf/integrity_sweep_adaptation.rs` — performance tests
- `artifacts/10.14/sweep_policy_trajectory.csv` — trajectory telemetry
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1fp4/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable pass/fail evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1fp4/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary

## Dependencies
- Upstream: bd-1daz (retroactive hardening — provides repairability scores for trajectory), bd-1ayu (hardening clamps — escalation rate limits affect sweep scheduling)
- Downstream: bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (10.14 plan gate)","acceptance_criteria":"Sweep cadence adjusts according to policy evidence bands; escalation/de-escalation hysteresis prevents oscillation; decisions are ledgered.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:56.380775372Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T19:52:12.079878306Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T19:52:12.079842189Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fp4","depends_on_id":"bd-1ayu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:23:53.401927623Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1fp4","depends_on_id":"bd-1daz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:14.852197185Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

+{"id":"bd-1fxuv","title":"Fail closed on RemoteCap endpoint-prefix vector collisions","description":"Why this exists: crates/franken-node/src/security/remote_cap.rs::scope_fingerprint() and canonical_payload() collapse scope.endpoint_prefixes with raw comma joins before deriving token_id and the signed HMAC payload. Because endpoint_prefixes are arbitrary strings, materially different endpoint-prefix vectors can alias to the same fingerprint/payload surface when commas appear inside a prefix, even though RemoteCap treats the scope as a structured vector. What this task must do: preserve endpoint-prefix vector boundaries in the RemoteCap token_id and signed payload derivation, add focused regressions proving distinct endpoint vectors no longer collide, and validate the narrow surface with rch-only cargo runs. Acceptance criteria: 1. materially different endpoint_prefixes vectors that previously alias now produce different token IDs and/or signatures; 2. identical inputs remain deterministic and valid tokens still authorize correctly; 3. cargo-heavy validation is done with rch and reported in Agent Mail.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"DustyAnchor","created_at":"2026-03-15T15:35:08.912569582Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-15T15:35:35.578778041Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["endpoint-prefix","remote-cap","scope-binding","security"]}

 {"id":"bd-1ga5","title":"[10.21] Implement cohort-aware baseline modeling for expected phenotype evolution patterns.","description":"## Why This Exists

Not all phenotype evolution is anomalous -- extensions naturally evolve as they add features, refactor code, and update dependencies. To distinguish genuine compromise precursors from benign evolution, BPET needs cohort-aware baselines: models of what "normal" evolution looks like for comparable extensions.

This bead implements cohort baseline modeling that groups extensions by relevant similarity dimensions (domain, maturity stage, release cadence, dependency class) and computes expected evolution patterns with confidence envelopes. An extension's trajectory is only flagged as anomalous if it deviates from its cohort's baseline beyond the confidence envelope -- reducing false positives from natural evolution patterns.

Within the 9O enhancement map, cohort baselines are consumed by the drift feature engine (bd-2ao3) and regime-shift detector (bd-2lll) as reference distributions for anomaly scoring.

## What This Must Do

1. Define cohort classification dimensions: domain category, maturity stage (new, established, legacy), release cadence (daily, weekly, monthly, irregular), dependency class (minimal, moderate, heavy).
2. Implement cohort assignment: classify each extension into the appropriate cohort based on its properties.
3. Compute expected evolution patterns per cohort: typical phenotype change rates, capability growth curves, dependency churn rates, complexity trajectories.
4. Generate confidence envelopes around cohort baselines: quantile-based bounds that define the range of "normal" evolution for each metric.
5. Ensure model recalibration is deterministic and versioned: recalibration from the same data produces the identical baseline model.
6. Support historical replay: baseline models can be recomputed from historical evidence for audit purposes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Baselines are generated for comparable extension cohorts (domain, maturity, release cadence, dependency class) and include confidence envelopes; model recalibration is deterministic and versioned.
- At least 4 cohort classification dimensions are implemented.
- Confidence envelopes are computed for each phenotype metric within each cohort.
- Recalibration from identical data produces byte-identical baseline models.
- Baseline model version identifiers are embedded and machine-parseable.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: cohort assignment correctness for known extension profiles; baseline computation for synthetic cohorts; confidence envelope calculation; recalibration determinism.
- Integration tests: full cohort modeling pipeline from extension histories to baseline models; baseline stability across recalibrations with identical data; sensitivity to cohort boundary changes.
- Structured logging: cohort modeling events with stable codes (BPET-COHORT-001 through BPET-COHORT-NNN); per-cohort baseline statistics; confidence envelope bounds; trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay: synthetic extension histories with known cohort assignments and baselines for CI verification.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/security/bpet/cohort_baselines.rs` -- cohort baseline implementation
- `tests/security/bpet_baseline_calibration.rs` -- calibration test suite
- `artifacts/10.21/bpet_cohort_baseline_report.json` -- sample baseline report
- `artifacts/section_10_21/bd-1ga5/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_21/bd-1ga5/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- bd-2xgs (blocks) -- [10.21] Implement deterministic phenotype feature extraction: provides the feature vectors that baselines are computed from","acceptance_criteria":"- Baselines are generated for comparable extension cohorts (domain, maturity, release cadence, dependency class) and include confidence envelopes; model recalibration is deterministic and versioned.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:07.941121959Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:09:03.770751352Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:09:03.770723109Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-21","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ga5","depends_on_id":"bd-2xgs","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:27.575255456Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

That leaves the standalone workspace replay surface weaker than the connector implementation and the current Python certification checker does not guard against that drift. What this task must do: switch the workspace replay capsule expected hash comparison to the module's constant-time helper, add regression coverage in the Python checker/unit tests so the hardening is enforced, and keep scope limited to the workspace capsule + checker surfaces.  {"id":"bd-pga7","title":"[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 13

Task Objective:
Ensure operator workflows can contain and explain high-severity incidents deterministically.

Execution Requirements:
- Make this requirement machine-verifiable and release-gated where applicable.
- Keep behavior self-documenting so future contributors do not need to re-open the master plan.

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for rule/contract logic and error conditions.
- Integration/E2E tests for workflow-level enforcement.
- Structured logs with stable codes and trace IDs.
- Reproducible artifacts that prove contract satisfaction.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Success and failure invariants for [13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation are explicitly quantified and machine-verifiable.
- Determinism requirements for [13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation are validated across repeated runs and adversarial perturbations.


Expected Artifacts:
- Machine-readable verification artifact with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_13/bd-pga7/verification_evidence.json, suitable for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_13/bd-pga7/verification_summary.md, linking implementation intent to measured validation outcomes.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- The implementation must deliver the exact capability named in the title, with no scope dilution and no silent feature omission.
- Validation artifacts must include capability-specific thresholds, pass/fail criteria, and reproducible evidence bundles tied to this bead ID.
- Program/section verification gates must be able to consume this bead's outputs without manual interpretation.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation" is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation" prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every production incident of severity >= high produces a deterministic replay artifact within 24 hours of detection.
2. Replay artifact enables full causal explanation: root cause, propagation path, blast radius, and containment timeline.
3. Containment is deterministic: given the same incident inputs, the containment system produces identical isolation decisions every time.
4. Incident explanation report is generated automatically from replay artifacts (no manual investigation required for initial triage).
5. Containment latency target: from detection to isolation <= 30 seconds for automated containment, <= 5 minutes for human-in-the-loop.
6. Post-incident verification: replay the incident with the fix applied and confirm the incident does not recur.
7. Evidence: incident_containment_log.json with per-incident: detection time, containment time, replay artifact path, explanation completeness score.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:39:34.584488977Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T23:27:26.227454438Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T23:27:26.227425333Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-pga7","depends_on_id":"bd-2a4l","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:25.400230188Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-phf","title":"[10.4] Implement ecosystem telemetry for trust and adoption metrics.","description":"## Why This Exists

The extension ecosystem generates trust and adoption signals that must be measured, tracked, and acted upon. Ecosystem telemetry provides the quantitative feedback loop that drives reputation scoring, certification decisions, policy tuning, and program success measurement (Section 13 targets). Without telemetry, the ecosystem operates blind: trust claims cannot be validated, adoption cannot be measured, and emerging threats cannot be detected early.

This bead implements the telemetry pipeline specifically for trust and adoption metrics in the extension ecosystem. It complements the per-extension trust card (bd-2yh) by providing aggregate, ecosystem-level views: how many extensions are at each certification level? What is the average time-to-quarantine? How quickly are revocations propagating? What is the adoption curve for new trust features?

## What This Must Do

1. Define the ecosystem telemetry schema: trust metrics (certification level distribution, revocation propagation latency, quarantine-to-resolution time, provenance coverage rate, reputation distribution) and adoption metrics (extensions published per period, extensions using each provenance level, operator trust-card query volume, policy override frequency).
2. Implement telemetry collection pipeline: privacy-respecting aggregation from node-level events to ecosystem-level metrics. Must respect operator privacy preferences and comply with the privacy envelope from 10.19.
3. Implement telemetry storage with time-series retention policy (granular recent data, aggregated historical data).
4. Implement telemetry query API for dashboards, operator copilot, and policy engines.
5. Implement anomaly detection on telemetry streams: sudden drops in provenance coverage, spikes in quarantine events, or reputation distribution shifts may indicate ecosystem-level attacks.
6. Implement telemetry export for program success criteria measurement (Section 13): compatibility corpus pass rate, migration velocity, compromise reduction metrics.
7. Define telemetry data governance: what is collected, what is aggregated, what is retained, and what is published.
8. Implement telemetry dashboards or dashboard-ready data endpoints for ecosystem health monitoring.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Telemetry schema covers all defined trust and adoption metric families.
- Telemetry collection is opt-in with clear data governance documentation.
- Privacy-preserving aggregation is verified: no individual extension or operator data leaks through aggregate metrics.
- Anomaly detection triggers on at least 5 defined ecosystem-level threat patterns.
- Telemetry query API supports time-range queries, aggregation levels, and metric filtering.
- Telemetry export produces machine-readable artifacts compatible with Section 13 success criteria measurement.
- Telemetry pipeline has bounded resource consumption (CPU, memory, storage) with configurable limits.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: telemetry aggregation correctness, privacy filter validation, anomaly detection threshold logic, time-series retention policy.
- Integration tests: event ingestion -> aggregation -> query pipeline, anomaly detection trigger -> alert emission.
- E2E tests: simulated ecosystem activity -> telemetry collection -> dashboard data -> anomaly detection.
- Adversarial tests: telemetry poisoning (injecting false metrics), privacy leakage through aggregate queries (differential privacy attacks), resource exhaustion through telemetry flooding.
- Structured logs: TELEMETRY_INGESTED, TELEMETRY_AGGREGATED, TELEMETRY_QUERY_SERVED, TELEMETRY_ANOMALY_DETECTED (with anomaly type and severity), TELEMETRY_EXPORT_GENERATED, TELEMETRY_PRIVACY_FILTER_APPLIED. All with trace IDs.

## Expected Artifacts

- `docs/specs/section_10_4/bd-phf_contract.md` — ecosystem telemetry spec
- `src/supply_chain/ecosystem_telemetry.rs` — Rust types for telemetry pipeline
- `scripts/check_ecosystem_telemetry.py` — verification script with `--json` and `self_test()`
- `tests/test_check_ecosystem_telemetry.py` — unit tests for verification script
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-phf/verification_evidence.json` — CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-phf/verification_summary.md` — outcome summary

## Dependencies

- No section-internal blockers (this is a cross-cutting telemetry layer).
- Blocks: bd-261k (section gate), bd-1xg (plan tracker)","acceptance_criteria":"1. Telemetry schema captures ecosystem-level metrics: extension install/uninstall rates, trust-level distribution across installed extensions, quarantine frequency, revocation event rates, certification level distribution, and publisher reputation tier distribution.
2. Adoption funnel metrics: discovery -> install -> activate -> retain, with per-extension and aggregate views.
3. Trust health metrics: percentage of extensions at each certification level, mean time from quarantine to resolution, revocation propagation latency percentiles, and stale-attestation rates.
4. Telemetry data is privacy-preserving: no personally identifiable information is collected. Extension-level metrics are aggregated to publisher level or above before export. Local-only mode is the default; opt-in for remote telemetry.
5. Telemetry export formats: machine-readable JSON for automated consumption, and human-readable summary via CLI `franken-node ecosystem stats`.
6. Metric namespace follows the stable namespace convention from 10.13 (bd-1ugy): all ecosystem metrics use the `fnode.ecosystem.*` prefix with versioned schema.
7. Telemetry retention policy: local telemetry retained for configurable period (default 90 days); older data is summarized into aggregate snapshots before pruning.
8. Dashboard-ready: telemetry output includes all fields needed to populate a trust-health dashboard without additional transformation.
9. All telemetry operations emit structured log events: TELEMETRY_COLLECTED, TELEMETRY_EXPORTED, TELEMETRY_PRUNED with metric counts and trace IDs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:45.982792949Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T20:16:38.516984650Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T20:16:38.516949405Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-4","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-phf","depends_on_id":"bd-ml1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:16:51.966199093Z","created_by":"CrimsonCrane","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-pnpe","title":"Deep audit sweep: defense-in-depth .sum() overflow guards","notes":"CrimsonCrane: Fixed 5 remaining .sum() overflow risks in production code:
- obligation_tracker: total_leaks sum → saturating_add fold
- cancellation_injection: total_test_cases sum → saturating_add fold
- revocation_registry: total_revocations sum → saturating_add fold
- lane_scheduler: total_active sum → saturating_add fold
- trust_complexity: total_depth sum → saturating_add fold
- cancellation_protocol: held_count sum → saturating_add fold

All remaining .sum() calls are either f64 (can't overflow) or in test code. 7368+ tests pass.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:51:26.221973213Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:07:42.805095527Z","closed_at":"2026-03-06T04:07:42.805067865Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-q92s","title":"Ignore non-finite ecosystem telemetry values","description":"TelemetryPipeline in supply_chain/ecosystem_telemetry.rs accepts raw NaN/inf values and then averages them directly in detect_anomalies() and export_health()/compute_metric_avg(). +def check_impl_integrity_hash_covers_retention_fields() -> dict[str, Any]:
+    """C35: Integrity hash covers storage and retention timing fields."""
+    if not IMPL.is_file():
+        return _check("impl_integrity_hash_covers_retention_fields", False, "impl file missing")
+    content = IMPL.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+    required_lines = [
+        "hasher.update(bundle.size_bytes.to_le_bytes());",
+        "hasher.update(bundle.created_at_epoch.to_le_bytes());",
+        "hasher.update(bundle.last_tier_change_epoch.to_le_bytes());",
+        "bundle.integrity_hash = compute_integrity_hash(bundle);",
+    ]
+    missing = [line for line in required_lines if line not in content]
+    passed = len(missing) == 0
+    detail = "retention-affecting fields covered" if passed else f"missing: {missing}"
+    return _check("impl_integrity_hash_covers_retention_fields", passed, detail)
+
+
